sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 2 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: MOUSE(row=58, col=24), MOUSE(row=22, col=22).
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 22, level 2.
Valid actions right now: SPACE, MOUSE, ACTION7.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current knowledge ledger from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE LEDGER.
Pass it as the `world_model` argument of the `python` tool call, alongside `code`. That argument is the one that carries forward, so send it on every call, even when you reply with a tool call and no assistant text.
Game-tier fields (action_semantics, object_taxonomy, hud_map, win_pattern, level_log, cross_level_notes) persist across levels; level-tier fields (world_model, goal_model, recent_findings, open_questions, current_plan, strategy_log, failed_probes) reset when a level ends. Record every unproductive probe in failed_probes so it is never repeated, and tag claims VERIFIED(step N) or HYPOTHESIS.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
The `world_model` tool argument is the reliable way to update the ledger. Assistant text is optional; if you write any, keep it short and prefix lines with `Action semantics:`, `Object taxonomy:`, `HUD map:`, `Win pattern:`, `Level log:`, `Cross-level notes:`, `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, or `Failed probes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 2: you have used 6 of about 28 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Looking at the ASCII output, I can see the structure more clearly:
- Row 22: `cNNNNcccggcccNccNcccggcccR`
- The 'c' at col 34 is actually part of the red rectangle border, not a separate dot
- The red rect has dots at cols 22 (N-painted), 28 (g-gray), 34 (c-charcoal - this is actually a dot, different from gray), 40 (g-gray)
Wait, looking more carefully:
- `cNNNNcccggcccNccNcccggcccR`
- Positions: c(0) NNNN(1-4) ccc(5-7) gg(8-9) ccc(10-12) N(13) cc(14-15) N(16) ccc(17-19) gg(20-21) ccc(22-24) R(25)
So relative to the row start (col 20 in the game):
- Col 20: c (border)
- Col 21-24: NNNN (the painted green dot at col 21-24, but segmentation says col 21)
Wait, the segmentation said the N dot is at row 21, col 21 with pixels=16. So it's a 4x4 square.
Let me re-read the ascii:
Row 22 starts at game col 20:
c N N N N c c c g g c c c N c c N c c c g g c c c R
So the dots are:
- Col 21-24: N (painted green, 4x4)
- Col 28-29: gg (gray dot)
- Col 34-35: Nc or cN... wait let me count more carefully
Row 22: c(0) N(1) N(2) N(3) N(4) c(5) c(6) c(7) g(8) g(9) c(10) c(11) c(12) N(13) c(14) c(15) N(16) c(17) c(18) c(19) g(20) g(21) c(22) c(23) c(24) R(25)
Game col = 20 + index
- Index 1-4 (game col 21-24): N = painted green dot (4x4)
- Index 8-9 (game col 28-29): gg = gray dot
- Index 13 (game col 33): N = part of green T
- Index 16 (game col 36): N = part of green T  
- Index 20-21 (game col 40-41): gg = gray dot
Wait, that's only 3 dots in the red rect area. But the segmentation found 4-pixel objects at cols 28, 34, 40. Let me re-examine.
Actually the 'c' at col 34 (game col 34) is a 4-pixel object according to segmentation. But in the ASCII, row 22 at that position shows 'N' (index 13, game col 33). That's confusing.
Let me look at row 23 too:
Row 23: cNNNNcccggcccNccNcccggcccR
Same pattern. So the dots are 2x2 (gg = 2 wide, 2 tall = 4 pixels).
Looking at the segmentation output:
- color=g, pixels=4, row=22, col=28 (gray dot)
- color=c, pixels=4, row=22, col=34 (charcoal dot)
- color=g, pixels=4, row=22, col=40 (gray dot)
So there are 3 unpainted dots in the red rect: at cols 28, 34, 40. The one at col 34 is 'c' (charcoal) instead of 'g' (gray). This might be because it's partially overlapped by the green T shape, or it's a different type of dot.
Looking at the image, the red rect has 4 dot positions, with the first one painted green. The green T shape passes through the middle, between dots 2 and 3. The dot at position 3 (col 34) appears to be a darker gray (charcoal) - maybe it's behind the T shape or something.
For the green rect, the image shows: R, p, gray, gray.
So remaining to paint:
- Red rect: cols 28, 34, 40 (3 dots)
- Green rect: cols 34, 40 (2 dots)
Total: 5 dots, 4 colors available [O, M, b, Y]
I think maybe the 'c' dot doesn't need painting (it's already a different state), or maybe I need to paint it too. Let me just try painting all remaining gray dots and submit.
Actually, let me reconsider the whole puzzle. Maybe the goal isn't to match the top row at all. Maybe the green rect should be painted with colors that match the bottom row, and the red rect should be painted with colors from the top row? Or maybe the green T indicates that the green rectangle is "connected" to the top green square, meaning those dots should be green?
Let me just try a simple approach: paint all remaining gray dots with the available colors in order, then SPACE to submit. If wrong, I'll learn.
Remaining gray dots:
- Red rect col 28: paint with O
- Red rect col 40: paint with M
- Green rect col 34: paint with b
- Green rect col 40: paint with Y
That uses all 4 remaining colors. The 'c' dot at col 34 in red rect might not need painting.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Remaining gray dots: red rect cols 28, 40; green rect cols 34, 40
# Bottom colors: [O, M, b, Y]
# Paint them all and submit

# Paint red rect col 28 with O (bottom col 30)
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 58, 'col': 31}])
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 22, 'col': 28}])

# Paint red rect col 40 with M (bottom col 37)
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 58, 'col': 38}])
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 22, 'col': 40}])

# Paint green rect col 34 with b (bottom col 44)
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 58, 'col': 45}])
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 36, 'col': 34}])

# Paint green rect col 40 with Y (bottom col 51)
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 58, 'col': 52}])
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 36, 'col': 40}])

# Check state
seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("All dots:")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['pixels'] == 4:
        print(f"  color={n['color']}, row={n['boundary'][0][0]}, col={n['boundary'][0][1]}")

# Submit
result = action(['SPACE'])
print(f"SPACE: level_completed={last_action_result.get('level_completed', False)}, done={last_action_result.get('done', False)}")
</parameter>
<parameter=world_model>
{"game_tier": {"action_semantics": {"SPACE": "Submits solution", "MOUSE": "Select bottom color, click dot to paint it"}, "object_taxonomy": {"top_panel": "Target pattern [O,p,R,b,N,Y,M]", "red_rect": "4 dots at row 22", "green_rect": "4 dots at row 36", "bottom_panel": "Paint palette"}, "hud_map": {"timer_bar": "row ~53"}, "win_pattern": {"paint_dots_then_space": "Paint all dots, SPACE to submit"}, "level_log": {"level_1": "Paint 4 dots, SPACE to submit"}, "cross_level_notes": {}}, "level_tier": {"world_model": "Level 2: Painting dots in both red and green rects. 3 painted so far.", "goal_model": "Paint all dots to match some pattern", "recent_findings": ["Red rect col 34 dot is charcoal 'c', not gray"], "open_questions": ["What is target pattern?"], "current_plan": "Paint remaining 4 gray dots with 4 remaining colors, then SPACE", "strategy_log": [], "failed_probes": []}}
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
